jokes.text: And, when we have a good quarter... fireworks. Or a race car. Doesn't have to be a race car. Use your imagination.
jokes.setup: Michael proposes graphics for quarterly reports to make them less boring
jokes.punchline: His examples devolve from fireworks to race car, then he says it doesn't have to be a race car while clearly having no other ideas
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael - confidently offering vague suggestions while pretending there's a bigger vision. The self-contradiction is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: There's this cube on the screen, and it bounces around all day. And sometimes it looks like it's heading right into the corner of the screen and at the last minute it hits the wall and bounces away. We are all just dying to see it go right into the corner.
jokes.setup: Jim describes the DVD screensaver bouncing around
jokes.punchline: The entire office is invested in seeing it hit the corner perfectly
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures the mundane obsessions of office life. Became a cultural reference point for workplace boredom.

jokes.text: Pam claims that she saw it one day when she was alone in the conference room. Okay... I believe she thinks she saw it.
jokes.setup: Discussion of whether Pam actually saw the DVD logo hit the corner
jokes.punchline: Jim's diplomatic way of calling Pam delusional
jokes.explanation: Jim's careful phrasing shows his diplomatic nature while still being skeptical. The structure builds perfectly.

jokes.text: I saw it. I saw it, and it was amazing. Who said I didn't see it? Did Jim say that? I saw it!
jokes.setup: Jim's diplomatic doubt about Pam seeing the DVD corner hit
jokes.punchline: Pam's increasingly defensive insistence becomes more unconvincing
jokes.explanation: Pam's defensiveness reveals she probably didn't see it. The repetition and escalation work well.

jokes.text: All r-- All right. Let's quit while we're ahead. - That was so awesome. - That was awesome. Thank you. Some days, I am just on fire. What can I say?
jokes.setup: Michael ends what appears to be a failed brainstorming session
jokes.punchline: He declares it awesome and himself 'on fire' despite producing nothing useful
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael delusion - thinking his terrible ideas were brilliant. Very much in character.

jokes.text: Can you write where I can read it? - Yep. I'll read this when I get home.
jokes.setup: Michael asks Meredith to sign his cast in a readable spot
jokes.punchline: He immediately says he'll read it later, implying she wrote it somewhere he can't see
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag that works through implication. Michael's immediate contradiction is mildly funny.

jokes.text: Attention, earthlings. I have some news. Today's the big day that I'm heading to New York to attend a party with sushi and important people.
jokes.setup: Michael makes an office announcement
jokes.punchline: 'Attention earthlings' treats his coworkers like aliens, then brags about sushi and important people
jokes.explanation: Michael's grandiose language and need to impress are perfectly in character. The 'earthlings' bit is absurd enough to work.

jokes.text: Well, the website is the brainchild of my brainchild, Ryan. It is my brain grandchild.
jokes.setup: Michael explains his relationship to Ryan and the website
jokes.punchline: Creates the nonsensical term 'brain grandchild' while trying to sound important
jokes.explanation: Michael's attempt to create familial terminology for business relationships is absurd but perfectly him. Economical and memorable.

jokes.text: And I do not mean policemen.
jokes.setup: Michael mentions partying with 'New York City's finest'
jokes.punchline: Clarifies he doesn't mean police, revealing his attempt at sophistication
jokes.explanation: Michael's need to clarify his sophisticated reference shows his insecurity about being misunderstood. Simple but effective.

jokes.text: That's from Ryan? Does it mention if he's seeing anybody? No. I'll find out tonight. Yes, please let us know.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Ryan's press release
jokes.punchline: Kelly immediately pivots to Ryan's relationship status with Michael enabling her obsession
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly moment - completely ignoring business to focus on Ryan gossip. Michael's enabling is also in character.

jokes.text: Dwight mercy killed Angela's cat. It's very complicated. It's caused a lot of unpleasantness between Dwight and Angela. Who were both already prone to unpleasantness.
jokes.setup: Jim explains the Dwight-Angela tension
jokes.punchline: Understates that they were 'already prone to unpleasantness' - massive understatement
jokes.explanation: Jim's diplomatic understatement is perfect - calling two of the most unpleasant people 'prone to unpleasantness' is masterful restraint.

jokes.text: Stanley, you're dancing. No, I'm not.
jokes.setup: Michael observes Stanley apparently dancing
jokes.punchline: Stanley's flat denial while presumably still moving
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stanley - caught doing something he doesn't want to admit. The economy is excellent - two lines, complete joke.

jokes.text: Buying paper just became fun.
jokes.setup: Ryan's corporate buzzword-filled speech about the website
jokes.punchline: Claims buying paper is now 'fun' - obviously ridiculous
jokes.explanation: Ryan's corporate speak reaches absurd heights. The idea that buying paper could be fun is inherently ridiculous.

jokes.text: I can't tell if he's mocking me. - Just ignore him. Can't do that. It's really hard for me to let things go. I was. Mocking. Thank you.
jokes.setup: Dwight unsure if Jim is mocking him
jokes.punchline: Jim confirms he was mocking, and Dwight thanks him for the honesty
jokes.explanation: Perfect character dynamics - Dwight's need for clarity, Jim's honesty about his mockery, and Dwight's grateful response. The escalation works perfectly.

jokes.text: Go to my car. Open the trunk. Inside, you will see many pelts. Under the smallest one is a case. Inside that case is a bear horn. Bring it to me.
jokes.setup: Jim suggests a horn to celebrate Dwight's sales
jokes.punchline: Dwight has an elaborate setup involving pelts and a bear horn in his car trunk
jokes.explanation: Peak Dwight absurdity - the specific details (many pelts, smallest one, bear horn) paint a vivid picture of his bizarre preparedness.

jokes.text: So I googled 'how to deal with difficult people,' and I got all of this.
jokes.setup: Phyllis dealing with Angela's bad attitude
jokes.punchline: She literally googled how to deal with difficult people
jokes.explanation: Relatable modern solution to workplace conflict. Phyllis's practical approach is endearing and realistic.

jokes.text: Maybe you could just change the 'u' into an 'a'. Then it would say 'lanch' party, Kevin. Would it be better if it said 'lanch' party?
jokes.setup: Kevin suggests fixing the 'lunch party' banner typo
jokes.punchline: His solution creates 'lanch party' which Angela points out is nonsensical
jokes.explanation: Classic Kevin logic - his 'solution' makes things worse. Angela's exasperated response is perfect.

jokes.text: Easy, booster seat. Nobody cares about this party anyway.
jokes.setup: Kevin trying to help with party planning
jokes.punchline: Angela calls him 'booster seat' (height insult) and declares nobody cares
jokes.explanation: 'Booster seat' is a creative height insult that's mean but clever. Very Angela to be cruel and defeatist simultaneously.

jokes.text: Plan a party, Angela. Oh, and the entire world will see it. Oh! And here's $65 for your budget. And here are 4 idiots who'll do nothing but weigh you down. Oh, and your cat's still dead.
jokes.setup: Angela listing her party planning challenges
jokes.punchline: Escalates to the brutal reminder that her cat is dead
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation structure building to the darkest, most personal blow. Angela's self-pity reaches peak melodrama with the dead cat closer.

jokes.text: Who am I? (computer voice) You tell me. (computer voice) Not sure. Just became self-aware. So much to figure out.
jokes.setup: Jim pranking Dwight by pretending to be the website gained consciousness
jokes.punchline: The 'computer' claims to have just become self-aware with existential uncertainty
jokes.explanation: Classic Jim prank with sci-fi elements. The computer's existential crisis is both absurd and philosophically amusing.

jokes.text: I have been salesman of the month for 13 out of the last 12 months. You heard me right. I did so well last February that corporate gave me two plaques in lieu of a pay raise.
jokes.setup: Dwight boasting about his sales record
jokes.punchline: Claims 13 out of 12 months, then explains the impossible math, revealing corporate's cheapness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant mathematical impossibility that Dwight treats as an achievement. The punchline about two plaques instead of money adds corporate satire.

jokes.text: No-- No. No. If I go by myself, everybody'll think I'm a big loser. - Well... I've your permission to invite Carol? - What? Jesus, Michael! I'm sorry. It's just the first... girl that popped into my head. I'll find somebody I haven't slept with.
jokes.setup: Michael needs a date so he won't look like a loser
jokes.punchline: Suggests his ex Carol to Jan, then promises to find someone he hasn't slept with
jokes.explanation: Michael's tactlessness with Jan is peak cringe. His promise to find someone he hasn't slept with implies a limited dating pool in Scranton.

jokes.text: Look at that. They have their own little language now, like twins.
jokes.setup: Jim and Pam communicate nonverbally about who goes to New York
jokes.punchline: Michael compares their couple communication to twins having their own language
jokes.explanation: Sweet observation that captures couple dynamics. Michael's perspective as an outsider looking in on their relationship.

jokes.text: Here's a suggestion, computer. I assume you read binary. So why don't you 011-11-011-011?
jokes.setup: Dwight getting frustrated with the 'computer'
jokes.punchline: Tries to curse at the computer in binary code
jokes.explanation: Dwight's attempt to insult a computer in its 'native language' is perfectly absurd. The specific binary sequence implies profanity.

jokes.text: Um, while you were typing that, I searched every database in existence and learned every fact about everything... And mastered the violin. Oh, and sold more paper.
jokes.setup: Dwight's binary insult took time to type
jokes.punchline: Computer claims to have learned everything and mastered violin in that time, plus sold more paper
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the computer's superhuman abilities. The violin detail is absurdly specific and the sales reminder maintains the prank's focus.

jokes.text: Phyllis, these are spoons. Spoons have round tops and are used to scoop things. What we need are forks which have prongs or tiny spears on top. And we need knives which have blades. Do you understand me now? Yes. Goody.
jokes.setup: Angela frustrated with Phyllis bringing spoons instead of mixed cutlery
jokes.punchline: Explains cutlery to Phyllis like she's a child, ending with condescending 'Goody'
jokes.explanation: Peak Angela condescension. Her baby-talk explanation of basic cutlery is brutal, and 'Goody' is perfectly dismissive.

jokes.text: But when you put paper in the shopping cart, it says, 'Thanks for shopping with Dunder Mifflin.' Damn it, Kelly, it knows! It knows what you did!
jokes.setup: Dwight discovers Kelly ordered paper online
jokes.punchline: Treats the website's standard thank-you message as evidence of artificial intelligence
jokes.explanation: Dwight's paranoia about the website reaches absurd heights. His interpretation of basic e-commerce features as AI consciousness is perfect.

jokes.text: I am not a bad person. When I left Staples, I took some of their leads with me, but I never intended to use them. What did intend to do with them? Who knows? Maybe keep them as a souvenir. Maybe use them.
jokes.setup: Jim justifying taking leads from his previous job
jokes.punchline: Claims he didn't intend to use them, then immediately admits he might use them
jokes.explanation: Jim's rationalization falls apart immediately. His honesty about his contradictory thinking is endearing and realistic.

jokes.text: Good-Bye. I love you. I love you-- Okay. I'm leaving inside Jim's car I don't know when I'll be back again Yes, I do Tomorrow I'll be back I'll be back tomorrow
jokes.setup: Michael dramatically saying goodbye to Pam
jokes.punchline: Builds up emotional farewell then immediately admits he'll be back tomorrow, singing it like a song
jokes.explanation: Michael's dramatic exit deflated by reality. The musical quality and self-correction work well together.

jokes.text: Oh. I didn't realize we could use the leads we stole from Staples. (computer voice)
jokes.setup: Jim using stolen leads from Staples
jokes.punchline: The 'computer' innocently reveals Jim's theft, escalating the prank
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that serves the prank. The computer's 'innocent' revelation of Jim's theft adds stakes to the competition.

jokes.text: What are you doing? That is an invitation to an online party. No. I'm sure that's not-- Are there... three 'Ws' at the beginning of the address? - Yes. - Yep.
jokes.setup: Michael realizes his VIP invitation might be fake
jokes.punchline: The three Ws confirm it's just a website, not an exclusive party
jokes.explanation: Michael's dawning realization is painful to watch. His hope being crushed by basic internet knowledge is peak cringe.

jokes.text: Doesn't it piss you off sometimes that little twerp got the promotion over us? Oh, actually I withdrew from consideration. Yeah, I withdrew too.
jokes.setup: Jim asks if Michael is angry about Ryan's promotion
jokes.punchline: Both claim they withdrew from consideration for the job they obviously wanted
jokes.explanation: Classic face-saving behavior. Both men pretending they didn't want something they clearly wanted shows their wounded pride.

jokes.text: After numerous projections that the computer would crush all salesmen in its path, I am very happy to report that our very own Dwight Schrute has crushed his electronic nemesis, if you will, by a whopping 52... reams! Say it again-- Announcing it. 52 reams! No, no, the first part. Dwight has defeated the computer!
jokes.setup: Michael announces Dwight's victory over the website
jokes.punchline: His dramatic announcement needs correction and repetition, Dwight wants the ego boost repeated
jokes.explanation: Michael's over-the-top announcement and Dwight's need for validation work well together. The coaching of the announcement adds meta-humor.

jokes.text: How do you tell someone it's over? You send them a notarized letter, right? Well, what if the recipient is your notary?
jokes.setup: Angela asking about ending a relationship formally
jokes.punchline: The person she wants to break up with is her notary, creating a practical problem
jokes.explanation: Brilliant premise that's perfectly Angela - wanting to break up formally but creating a logistical nightmare. The structure is flawless.

jokes.text: Hey, do you have any men that you can fix me up with? I would like to have a relationship with a man. I'll get back to you. Let me know.
jokes.setup: Angela asking Pam for dating help
jokes.punchline: Her robotic phrasing about wanting 'a relationship with a man' and the awkward exchange
jokes.explanation: Angela's formal, clinical approach to dating is perfectly in character. Her robotic phrasing makes romance sound like a business transaction.

jokes.text: You beat me. You are the superior being. (computer voice)
jokes.setup: The computer prank concludes with Dwight's victory
jokes.punchline: The 'computer' admits defeat in grandiose terms
jokes.explanation: Good conclusion to the prank that feeds Dwight's ego. The grandiose language matches the sci-fi premise.

jokes.text: Beer, light beer, streamers, orchids, better lighting, something made of ice. Pizza with mushrooms, pizza without mushrooms, white pi-- I would like this party to be sexier, cooler, more important-- Chocolates, someone famous, cool music, confetti, go-go dancers? I want it to embarrass all other parties.
jokes.setup: Michael listing party requirements for Angela
jokes.punchline: List escalates from reasonable to absurd (go-go dancers) and wanting to 'embarrass all other parties'
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from mundane to ridiculous. Michael's competitive need to have the best party drives the absurdity.

jokes.text: You hadn't noticed she's a woman? I hear she's single and ready to mingle. I'm thinking about making a play for her. What do you think? Crazy, right?
jokes.setup: Michael asking Toby about dating Angela
jokes.punchline: Uses 'single and ready to mingle' unironically and asks if pursuing Angela is crazy
jokes.explanation: Michael's cliché dating language and obliviousness to how inappropriate this would be. The cringe comes from his genuine excitement.

jokes.text: Michael, there's a very big difference between these two pizza places. Both in quality of ingredients and in overall taste. Which one did you order from? Pizza by Alfredo. All right, you know what? Okay, okay. What is better, a medium amount of good pizza, or all you can eat of pretty good pizza? A medium amount of good pizza.
jokes.setup: Confusion between two pizza places and Michael's philosophical question
jokes.punchline: Everyone chooses quality over quantity, implying Michael ordered terrible pizza
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the pizza disaster. Michael's philosophical framing can't hide his terrible choice.

jokes.text: Oh, no, it's bad. It's real bad. It's like eating a hot circle of garbage.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Pizza by Alfredo's quality
jokes.punchline: Kevin's vivid description of it as 'a hot circle of garbage'
jokes.explanation: 'Hot circle of garbage' is a perfect, memorable description. The visual is vivid and the phrasing is both accurate and poetic.

jokes.text: They say you should never mix business with pleasure. Really? Well then explain to me how a putt-putt golf company operates.
jokes.setup: Michael questioning the business/pleasure advice
jokes.punchline: Uses mini golf as proof you can mix business with pleasure, missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Michael's logic is completely wrong but delivered with such confidence. His misunderstanding of the saying is perfect.

jokes.text: It's about to get all stupid up in here!
jokes.setup: Michael threatening the pizza delivery kid
jokes.punchline: His attempt at intimidation is 'It's about to get all stupid up in here'
jokes.explanation: Michael's attempt at tough guy talk is hilariously ineffective. 'All stupid' as a threat is perfectly Michael.

jokes.text: I've seen this kid, he sneaks onto my farm and steals my hemp. Yeah, I know that guy. He's that farmer that grows really crappy weed.
jokes.setup: Creed recognizes the pizza delivery kid
jokes.punchline: Mutual recognition - Creed grows hemp, kid steals it and insults the quality
jokes.explanation: Peak Creed moment - casual admission of drug cultivation met with criticism of quality. The mutual recognition adds perfect absurdity.

jokes.text: Listen up, kid. I don't like you, but because some town in Switzerland says so, you have rights.
jokes.setup: Michael addressing the hostage pizza kid
jokes.punchline: References Geneva Convention as 'some town in Switzerland says so'
jokes.explanation: Michael's dismissive reference to international human rights law as 'some town in Switzerland' is brilliantly ignorant and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Here you are, my dear. One thing made of ice. How did you... Where-- Where did you... It's just ice. It'll melt all over the floor. - Will you help me put it over there? - Yes, I will. Excuse me. I stole it.
jokes.setup: Creed delivers Angela's requested ice sculpture
jokes.punchline: Casual admission that he stole a block of ice
jokes.explanation: Classic Creed - helpful but through illegal means, delivered with complete nonchalance. His honesty about theft is perfectly Creed.

jokes.text: I just think you should know that one of my salesmen beat your stupid computer. So take that, asshole. Always a jokester.
jokes.setup: Michael gets his moment on the webcast to New York
jokes.punchline: Insults Ryan and the website on live stream, Ryan covers with 'always a jokester'
jokes.explanation: Michael's petty revenge moment is perfectly cringe. Ryan's damage control response shows the awkwardness of the situation.

jokes.text: If anyone out there is listening, I'm being held here against my will. I'm a minor.
jokes.setup: Pizza kid appears on the webcast
jokes.punchline: Calls for help on live stream, emphasizing he's a minor to make it worse
jokes.explanation: The kidnapping reaches its peak absurdity with public exposure. The minor detail makes the legal implications worse.

jokes.text: If you're going number one, you've got ten more seconds!
jokes.setup: Dwight guarding the pizza kid
jokes.punchline: Times bathroom breaks like a prison guard
jokes.explanation: Dwight's authoritarian approach to hostage management. The specificity of timing bathroom breaks is absurd but in character.

jokes.text: If you change your mind I'll be first in line Honey, I'm still free Take a chance on me
jokes.setup: Andy's romantic pursuit of Angela
jokes.punchline: Serenades her with ABBA while she's clearly uninterested
jokes.explanation: Andy's oblivious romantic gesture is painful to watch. The song choice and timing are perfectly inappropriate.

jokes.text: I'm Ryan, and... tonight didn't go the way that I thought it would. Because... it didn't work out for me. I'm very embarrassed. I have egg all over my face. And... I'm 12 years old.
jokes.setup: Jim and Dwight impersonating Ryan's confession
jokes.punchline: Mock confession includes 'I'm 12 years old' as the ultimate insult
jokes.explanation: Good mockery of Ryan's corporate speak and arrogance. The '12 years old' line captures his immaturity perfectly.
